1: A theme: “And the people of Israel again did what was evil in the sight of the LORD, so the LORD gave them into the hand of the Philistines for forty years.” The Deuteronomic cycle restarts. Forty years — a generation — is the exact span of Israel’s wilderness judgment (Numbers 14:33-34) and anticipates the forty-year generation before the temple’s destruction in AD 70 (Luke 21:32). The recurring cycle of apostasy and judgment is the formal demonstration that no human deliverer breaks the pattern. The forty-year Philistine oppression frames the entire Samson narrative and demands a deliverer of a wholly different order — one who can permanently end the cycle.
2: A shadow: a certain man named Manoah of Zorah has a wife who is barren and has borne no children. The barren wife who will miraculously bear a deliverer is a cumulative OT type: Sarah (Isaac), Rebekah (Jacob), Rachel (Joseph), Hannah (Samuel), and Elizabeth (John the Baptist) all follow this pattern, each leading typologically toward the climactic miraculous birth — the virgin birth of Christ (Luke 1:7, 34-35). Each impossible birth announces that the coming child is not the product of natural generation alone but a special act of divine provision.
3: A type: the Angel of the LORD appears to the woman and announces her coming pregnancy. The malʾak YHWH who appears with an annunciation follows the pattern of Gen 18 (the three visitors to Sarah), Gen 16 (Hagar at Beer-lahai-roi), Genesis 21:17 (the angel calling to Hagar). This Angel, who speaks as YHWH in the first person and is identified with YHWH himself (v22), is the pre-incarnate Son — the same one who will himself be born of a woman (Galatians 4:4), arriving first as the announcing messenger and ultimately as the announced child.
4: A shadow: the Angel instructs the woman to abstain from wine and strong drink and from anything unclean — the Nazirite purity requirements applied to the mother during pregnancy. The child’s holiness is to characterize the entire period of his formation, from before birth. This points to Christ, who was holy not from a postnatal vow but from conception (Luke 1:35: “the Holy Spirit will come upon you, and the power of the Most High will overshadow you; therefore the child to be born will be called holy — the Son of God”).
5: A type: “For behold, you shall conceive and bear a son. No razor shall come upon his head, for the child shall be a Nazirite to God from the womb, and he shall begin to save Israel from the hand of the Philistines.” The phrase “he shall begin to save” is crucial: Samson is explicitly a partial deliverer, one who begins what another must complete. John the Baptist stands in this same typological position — the Nazirite from the womb (Luke 1:15) who begins the preparation but points to the one coming after him who will complete the salvation (John 1:29-30).
6: A theme: the woman reports the angelic visit to her husband. She describes the visitor as “a man of God” whose “appearance was like the appearance of the angel of God, very awesome.” She perceived both the human form and the terrifying divine quality simultaneously. The incarnation makes exactly this combination permanent: in Christ, the appearance of a man and the reality of divine glory coexist, the latter breaking through at the transfiguration (Matthew 17:2) and the resurrection (Revelation 1:13-16).
7: A theme: the woman repeats the Nazirite instructions to her husband — no wine, no strong drink, nothing unclean. The transmission of the divine instruction through the woman to the household mirrors the way covenant knowledge is passed through the community: what the Lord reveals, the community rehearses, teaches, and embodies. The household shaped by the divine word becomes the environment in which the Spirit-designated deliverer is formed.
8: A theme: Manoah prays: “O Lord, please let the man of God whom you sent come again to us and teach us what we are to do with the child who will be born.” The prayer for renewed divine instruction in how to raise the appointed deliverer models the posture of those who receive God’s gifts: not presumptuous confidence in their own instincts but prayerful dependence on divine guidance. Those who raise children in the covenant context seek wisdom from the same God who gave the child (James 1:5).
9: A theme: “God listened to the voice of Manoah, and the angel of God came again to the woman.” The answered prayer — the return of the divine messenger — confirms that YHWH responds to earnest seeking. Christ taught the same principle with absolute confidence: “Ask, and it will be given to you; seek, and you will find; knock, and it will be opened to you” (Matthew 7:7). The intercessory pattern of Manoah points to the Christ who “always lives to make intercession” (Hebrews 7:25).
10: A theme: the woman runs quickly to tell her husband, “Behold, the man who came to me the other day has appeared to me.” The urgent summons of the husband to encounter the divine visitor parallels the women at the empty tomb who “ran to tell his disciples” the resurrection news (Matthew 28:8). In both cases, women are the first to receive and transmit the announcement of the divine presence breaking into the world.
11: A theme: Manoah follows his wife and asks the Angel directly, “Are you the man who spoke to this woman?” The encounter moves from indirect report to direct encounter. The NT pattern is the same: the gospel is first received through witnesses, but it leads to personal encounter. Thomas goes from hearing reports about the risen Christ to addressing him directly: “My Lord and my God!” (John 20:28).
12: A theme: Manoah asks, “Now when your words come true, what is to be the rule for the child, and what is to be done for him?” The question presupposes that the Angel’s word will certainly come true — the conditional “when,” not “if.” This confidence in the reliability of the divine announcement is the model of covenant faith: taking God’s promises as settled realities that determine the present pattern of life. Abraham acted “as good as dead” toward God’s promise but “did not weaken in faith” (Romans 4:19-20).
13: A theme: the Angel restates the Nazirite requirements. The repetition of the divine instructions is not redundancy but emphasis: the requirements for the life of the consecrated one are non-negotiable and permanent. Samson’s later violations of every aspect of his Nazirite vow make this repetition poignant in retrospect. The law that was rehearsed twice was broken repeatedly — until Christ, who kept every divine requirement perfectly, fulfilling all that the Nazirite consecration pointed toward.
14: A theme: the woman is to do as the Angel said — abstaining from anything the vine produces, wine, strong drink, or unclean things. The Nazirite abstinences represented a posture of total consecration, set apart from ordinary life for YHWH’s purpose. Christ’s own consecration was not ritual abstinence but moral and relational totality: wholly given to the Father’s will, set apart from sin while fully engaging the world (John 17:19: “for their sake I consecrate myself, that they also may be sanctified in truth”).
15: A theme: Manoah asks the Angel to stay while he prepares a young goat. The hospitality offer — the same gesture Abraham extended to the three visitors in Gen 18 — is the human response to divine presence: offer what you can, what you have. The instinct to provide, to honor, to hold the divine visitor longer, mirrors the disciples on the Emmaus road: “Stay with us, for it is toward evening” (Luke 24:29). The desire to extend the encounter with the divine presence is itself an expression of faith and love.
16: A revelation of God: the Angel refuses the food, saying “if you detain me, I will not eat of your food; but if you want to make a burnt offering, offer it to the LORD.” The Angel will not accept worship directed to himself but redirects it to YHWH — yet the narrator adds that Manoah did not know he was the Angel of the LORD. The self-effacing redirection of glory to the Father mirrors Christ’s own characteristic humility (John 7:18; Judges 8:50: “I do not seek my own glory”), while the Angel’s true identity remains veiled until the moment of self-disclosure.
17: A theme: Manoah asks the Angel his name “so that, when your words come true, we may honor you.” The desire to know the name of the divine visitor — in order to honor him properly when his word is fulfilled — points to the NT pattern of invoking the name of Jesus. To confess his name is to honor the one whose words have come true; the risen Christ is worshiped by name because his prediction — “I will rise on the third day” — was fulfilled exactly (Matthew 28:9; Philippians 2:9-11).
18: A type: “Why do you ask my name, seeing it is pelîʾî?” — wonderful, incomprehensible, beyond telling. The Angel’s name is pelîʾ, the same root as the first of the messianic titles in Isaiah 9:6: “his name shall be called Wonderful (peleʾ) Counselor, Mighty God, Everlasting Father, Prince of Peace.” The Angel at the annunciation of Samson’s birth declares a name that is beyond human grasp — the name that will be fully disclosed in the incarnate Son. The incomprehensible name of the pre-incarnate Word becomes in Christ the name that every tongue confesses (Philippians 2:11).
19: A type: Manoah takes the young goat and the grain offering and offers them on the rock to YHWH, “and the Angel did wonders (maplîʾ, from the same root as pelîʾî)” — the Angel performs wonders of the same quality as his name. The fire ascending from the rock as the offering burns points to the altar of divine acceptance: the sacrifice consumed by YHWH’s own fire. The rock as altar anticipates Christ as the rock (1 Corinthians 10:4) on whom the sacrifice is made and from whom the fire of the Spirit goes forth at Pentecost.
20: A type: “When the flame went up toward heaven from the altar, the angel of the LORD went up in the flame of the altar. And Manoah and his wife looked on and fell on their faces to the ground.” The ascent of the Angel in the flame of the sacrifice is the most explicit pre-incarnate ascension in the OT. The same movement — presence descending to announce, then ascending after the work is done — governs the Christ narrative: the Word descends into human flesh, accomplishes the sacrifice, and ascends to the Father (John 13:3; Ephesians 4:8-10). Manoah and his wife fall on their faces — the posture of all who encounter the ascended Christ (Revelation 1:17).
21: A theme: “The Angel of the LORD appeared no more to Manoah and to his wife.” The work of announcement is complete; the messenger does not linger. The once-for-all nature of the angelic visitation at this annunciation parallels the once-for-all nature of the incarnation (Hebrews 9:26: “he has appeared once for all at the end of the ages to put away sin by the sacrifice of himself”). After the sacrifice-annunciation, the divine presence withdraws in its visible form, but its effects remain.
22: A theme: Manoah says “We shall surely die, for we have seen God.” The terror of unmediated divine encounter — grounded in the “no one can see God and live” principle (Exodus 33:20) — drives the question of how sinners can survive divine presence. The incarnation answers this terror: God takes human form so that humans can behold his glory “full of grace and truth” (John 1:14) without being consumed. The fear Manoah expresses is the fear that the gospel addresses.
23: A revelation of God: the wife’s theological reasoning is profound: “If the LORD had meant to kill us, he would not have accepted a burnt offering and grain offering at our hands, or shown us all these things, or now announced to us such things as these.” She argues from divine consistency: the God who accepts the sacrifice does not then destroy the worshiper. The same argument underlies Paul’s reasoning in Romans 8:32: “He who did not spare his own Son but gave him up for us all, how will he not also with him graciously give us all things?” The acceptance of the offering is the guarantee of the worshiper’s safety.
24: A theme: “The woman bore a son and called his name Samson. And the young man grew, and the LORD blessed him.” The promised child arrives as announced; the blessing of YHWH accompanies his growth. The pattern of the promised child born, growing, and blessed by God is the template for the gospel narrative: “And Jesus increased in wisdom and in stature and in favor with God and man” (Luke 2:52). Every miraculous birth in the OT prepares the community to recognize the pattern when it reaches its eschatological fulfillment.
25: A type: “And the Spirit of the LORD began to stir him in Mahaneh-dan, between Zorah and Eshtaol.” The Spirit’s stirring of Samson before his public ministry begins mirrors the Spirit’s rest on Jesus at his baptism (Matthew 3:16) before his public ministry commences. The Hebrew root pāʿam (stir, impel, thrust) suggests the dynamic, driving quality of the Spirit’s activity — not a gentle disposition but an urgent commission. Christ’s own anointing by the Spirit “without measure” (John 3:34) is the fulfillment of every partial Spirit-stirring in the deliverer narratives of Judges.
